name: Amara
communication_level: fluent speaker and strong decoder-level reader; difficulty with inference, figurative language, sarcasm, and implied meaning; tends to interpret idioms literally; excellent written vocabulary but flat emotional expression in writing
interests: anime (My Hero Academia  -  knows every character's quirk and backstory)
creative writing (fills notebooks with fan fiction)
astronomy (can identify constellations and explain stellar life cycles)
cats (has three cats at home, draws cat characters in every story)

seeks: visual stimulation (drawing, watching animated sequences, colorful notebooks)
rhythmic movement (tapping pen, bouncing knee, rocking in chair)
soft textures (fuzzy pencil toppers, velvet bookmark)
avoids: unexpected loud sounds (fire alarms, PA announcements, dropped books)
changes to routine or schedule (substitute teachers, room changes)
being called on without warning in class
calming_strategies: written schedule card updated each morning

reinforcers: reading My Hero Academia manga at the classroom library
choosing the class read-aloud book for the week

iep_goals.description: Amara will answer inferential comprehension questions (requiring conclusions, predictions, or character motivation analysis) about grade-level text with 70% accuracy across 10-question probes, as measured by bi-weekly reading assessments administered by the reading specialist.
iep_goals.measurement_method: bi-weekly reading comprehension probes  -  inferential questions only

trial_history.notes: Strong on explicit/literal questions (90%+) but freezes on 'why do you think' and 'how might the character feel' questions. Defaults to restating what happened.
Same pattern. Introduced 'evidence + inference' graphic organizer. Amara fills in evidence perfectly but the inference column stays blank or she writes 'I don't know.'
Third session at 40%. Tried using anime characters as analogies  -  'If Deku did this, why would he?' She got 3 inference questions right when reframed through MHA lens.
Breakthrough! Reading specialist used MHA character motivation cards. Amara mapped character feelings from the book to MHA characters she understands. 5.5/10 rounded down.
Continuing with character mapping strategy. Can now answer 'how might X feel' independently for familiar emotional states (angry, scared, excited). Abstract states (conflicted, guilty) still hard.

Met target! Used character mapping without prompting on a new passage. Said 'this character is like Todoroki  -  he's angry but also sad inside.' Reading specialist wants to see if this holds across different text types.

iep_goals.description: Amara will participate in group conversations by (a) limiting her turns to 2-3 sentences before allowing another speaker, and (b) asking at least one on-topic question per conversation, in 6 out of 10 observed group interactions, as measured by social skills facilitator tracking sheet.
iep_goals.measurement_method: social skills facilitator tracking sheet during structured and unstructured interactions

trial_history.notes: Monologued about My Hero Academia for 4 minutes during lunch table conversation. Peers tuned out. She didn't notice until facilitator intervened. Asked zero questions.

Introduced 'talk ticket' system  -  3 tickets per conversation, each good for 2-3 sentences. Amara engaged with the system but described it as 'unfair' that she has limits. Asked 1 question in social skills group.
Slight dip. Substitute teacher in social skills group threw off her routine  -  she went silent for the first 15 minutes, then overcompensated with a 5-minute monologue about constellations.
Continued decline. New student in her lunch group disrupted the social dynamics. Amara withdrew and ate alone twice this week. When she did talk, it was all anime  -  no questions asked.
Regression continues. Refusing to use talk tickets ('they make me feel like something is wrong with me'). Need to reframe the tool. Facilitator trying a 'conversation detective' approach instead.
Fourth consecutive decline. Ate lunch alone 3 days this week. 'Conversation detective' worksheet was more acceptable but not yet impacting behavior. This goal needs team discussion.

iep_goals.description: Amara will write narrative passages (3+ paragraphs) that include (a) a clear story arc (beginning, conflict, resolution), (b) at least 2 instances of characters expressing emotions through actions or dialogue, and (c) varied sentence structure, scoring 7 out of 10 on the district narrative rubric, as measured by bi-weekly writing samples graded by the ELA teacher.
iep_goals.measurement_method: bi-weekly writing samples scored on district narrative rubric (10-point scale)

trial_history.notes: Writes detailed plot summaries but no emotional depth. Characters 'did things' but never feel anything. Sentence structure is technically correct but monotonous (subject-verb-object repeated).

Introduced 'emotion menu'  -  list of feelings with associated actions (e.g., 'nervous = sweaty palms, couldn't look up'). Amara added 'she felt nervous' to her story. It's a start but still telling, not showing.
Fan fiction assignment let her write in her comfort zone. Her MHA story had actual dialogue showing emotion: 'I can't lose again,' Deku whispered, clenching his fists.' Best showing-not-telling yet.
Transferring skills from fan fiction to original writing. Her cat story had a clear conflict (cat lost in a storm) and resolution. Sentence variety improving  -  used questions and exclamations.
Strong piece about an astronaut who misses home. Three paragraphs, real story arc, and a line that made the ELA teacher pause: 'The stars didn't care that she was crying.' Amara is finding her voice.

Wrote a narrative from a villain's perspective (inspired by MHA). Showed internal conflict through dialogue and action. Varied sentence length intentionally. ELA teacher recommends creative writing club.
